title: Why your supplier said no to the card

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner trying to pay Chinese suppliers with a credit card, you're going to get rejected. Here's why. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you offer Visa, your supplier hears 'I want to pay you with a tool you can't accept.' Then it gets worse. A credit card gives you 180 days to dispute the charge. On goods that already shipped. To a factory that already spent the money on materials. So for the supplier, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. Stop. The card isn't broken. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers on the same rails they already use. No 180-day chargeback risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

It's a loan with a return policy. The card isn't broken. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers on the same rails they already use. No 180-day chargeback risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Okay, picture this. You run a small business. You buy stuff from a factory in China. You try to pay with your credit card, and the supplier says no. You get frustrated. You think the supplier is being difficult. They're not. Here's what's really going on. Out of every 100 Chinese suppliers, only 3 to 5 of them can even take a credit card. The other 95 use apps called Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you hold up your Visa, your supplier is basically hearing, 'I want to pay you with something you can't use.' And it gets worse. With a credit card, you have 180 days to call the bank and take the money back. That's six months. But your supplier already bought the cloth, the metal, the parts. They already shipped your order. So if you call the bank in month five, the supplier loses everything. The order, the materials, the money. To them, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. The card isn't broken. It was just built for shopping at stores, not for paying factories. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier through the apps they use every day. No chargeback fear. No three percent fee they have to swallow. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Cold open + mechanism stack
scripts.original_vo: Watch this. You offered your supplier a credit card. They asked for T/T. You thought they were being difficult. They weren't. Here's the actual stack. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers have card acceptance set up. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. Most factories were never wired for Visa. Two. The 2.95% processing fee comes out of the supplier's margin, not yours. They're not paying that on a 50K order. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute, the money gets clawed back after materials are already cut. Four. Alipay and WeChat Pay are the standard. That's what every other buyer uses. Period. Stop blaming the supplier. The card was built for Amazon checkouts, not factory POs. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on the rails they already use. No 180-day risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Watch this. You wanted to pay your supplier with a credit card. They asked for a bank transfer instead. You thought they were being annoying. They weren't. Let me walk you through what's actually happening. First thing. Out of every 100 Chinese suppliers, only 3 to 5 are set up to take a credit card. The little card machines you see at coffee shops? Most Chinese factories don't have them. They were never wired up for that. Second thing. When you swipe a card, the supplier pays about three percent of the total to the card company. On a 50,000 dollar order, that's 1,500 dollars coming out of their pocket. Not yours. Third thing. The credit card gives you 180 days to call the bank and get your money back. That's six months. The factory has already bought the cloth, made the parts, shipped the boxes. If you call the bank later, they lose everything. Fourth thing. In China, almost everyone uses Alipay or WeChat Pay. That's what every other buyer in China pays with. So your card was built for buying stuff on Amazon, not for paying a real factory. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier using the same apps they already use every day. No six-month risk. No three percent fee they have to eat. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's what most business owners don't understand about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card isn't the problem. The category is. Credit cards were built for retail. You buy a sweater, you don't like it, you dispute, you get your money back. Beautiful. Now apply that to a factory order. You commission 10,000 units. The factory buys raw materials in week one. Cuts fabric in week three. Ships in week eight. You receive in week twelve. And the card gives you 180 days to dispute the whole thing. After materials are gone. After production is finished. After the ship has left port. No supplier on earth is going to accept that risk for a 2.95% fee they have to absorb. Stop. 95% of Chinese suppliers don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's the category. WorldFirst sends CNY direct on the same rails. No chargeback risk for the supplier. No 3% fee they have to swallow. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's something most small business owners miss about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card isn't the problem. The type of payment is. Let me explain. Credit cards were made for shopping. You buy a sweater at the store. You wear it once, decide you hate it, return it, get your money back. Lovely. Now picture a factory order instead. You order 10,000 t-shirts from a factory in China. Week one, the factory buys all the cloth and thread. Week three, they cut the fabric. Week eight, they pack the boxes and put them on a ship. Week twelve, the ship lands at your warehouse. Through all of that, your credit card gives you 180 days to call the bank and take the money back. That's after the cloth is bought. After the t-shirts are sewn. After the ship has left China. No factory in the world is going to take that risk for a three percent fee they have to pay out of their own pocket. And on top of that, almost no Chinese factories even have card machines. They use Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's how everyone in China pays everyone. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier on those same apps. No 180-day fear. No three percent fee for them. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Direct address + 3-thing stack
scripts.original_vo: If you've ever offered a credit card to a Chinese supplier and got told no, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening at once. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept Visa or Mastercard. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. The infrastructure isn't there. Two. The 2.95% processing fee gets eaten by the supplier, not you. On a 12K order that's 360 bucks coming out of their margin. They're not absorbing that. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute next quarter, they lose everything. Materials gone. Inventory gone. Their bank claws the money back. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. They're protecting their business. Stop trying to force the card. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to your supplier on Alipay, WeChat, and bank rails. The systems they actually use. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their side. If you're paying China, WorldFirst is the tool. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: I'll say it opinion-as-truth
scripts.original_vo: I'll say it. The credit card is the most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments and nobody wants to admit it. You offered Visa. Your supplier said T/T. You blamed the supplier. The supplier wasn't the problem. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card acceptance. 95% have run on Alipay or WeChat for a decade. The card isn't part of their world. Plus the supplier eats the 2.95% fee. Plus they carry 180 days of chargeback risk. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. WorldFirst pays them in CNY on the rails they actually use. No fee on their side. No 180-day risk. The card category was built for retail. Factory orders are a different game. Period.

Your supplier asked for a bank transfer instead. You blamed the supplier. But the supplier wasn't the problem. Let me break it down. Out of every 100 Chinese factories, only 3 to 5 can even take a credit card. The other 95 have been using Alipay or WeChat for over ten years. The credit card just isn't part of their world. On top of that, when you pay by card, the supplier has to pay about three percent to the card company. That comes out of their pocket. And on top of that, they carry six months of risk where you could call the bank and take the money back. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're basically asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. Why would anyone agree to that? At WorldFirst, we pay them in their own money through the apps they actually use. No fee on their side. No six-month risk. The credit card was built for shopping. Factory orders are a completely different game.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody's telling you
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built for the wholesale workflow. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama. Period.

Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built differently. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Nobody talks about this, but it changes how you think about paying Chinese suppliers. The credit card was never meant for this kind of payment. It was built for stuff like coffee shops, online stores, hotel rooms. Places where you buy something, you take it home or use it that day, and if it's broken you can call the bank to take it back. Easy. Now picture a factory order instead. The factory spends three months making your stuff. Then a month shipping it across the ocean. That's four months total. But your credit card gives you six whole months to call the bank and reverse the payment. So your supplier ships first, then sits there for five months wondering if your money is real. Meanwhile, only 3 to 5 out of every 100 Chinese factories even have card machines. The other 95 use Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a shopping tool onto a factory job. At WorldFirst, we built things differently. We send Chinese money straight to your supplier through the apps they already use. No chargeback drama. Just search WorldFirst.
scripts.diff_markup: Here's <del>the truth</del><ins>something</ins> nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is <del>built for the wholesale workflow</del><ins>built differently</ins>. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama<del>. Period</del>.

scripts.subtitle: Real talk + supplier's view
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You're treating the credit card like a payment method. Your supplier is treating it like a liability. You see 2.95% as a fee you'll happily eat. Your supplier sees 2.95% coming out of their margin because the merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days of their bank reversing the money on goods they already shipped. You see Visa. Your supplier sees a system 95% of their factory peers don't even use. The card isn't bad. It's just not for this. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The systems they actually use. Search WorldFirst. Now.
scripts.iterated_vo: For every business owner sourcing from China. You're treating the credit card like a payment method. Your supplier is treating it like a liability. You see 2.95% as a fee you'll happily eat. Your supplier sees 2.95% coming out of their margin because the merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days of their bank reversing the money on goods they already shipped. You see Visa. Your supplier sees a system 95% of their factory peers don't even use. The card isn't bad. It's just not for this. This happens every week. Every single week. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The systems they actually use. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: For every small business owner who buys stuff from China, here's the thing nobody tells you. You're looking at the credit card like a way to pay. Your supplier is looking at it like a risk to their business. Picture both sides at the same time. You see a three percent fee, and you're happy to eat it. Your supplier sees that same three percent coming out of their own pocket, because in card payments, the seller pays the fee, not the buyer. Most people don't even know that. You see protection if something goes wrong. Your supplier sees six months of fear where their bank could pull the money back on goods they already shipped. You see Visa, the brand you know. Your supplier sees a system that 95 percent of other factories around them don't even use. So the card isn't bad. It's just not made for this kind of deal. This same scene plays out every single week between buyers and Chinese suppliers. At WorldFirst, we pay Chinese suppliers in their own money through Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The same apps they use every day. Just search WorldFirst.

PH1.analysis: Core mechanism is real and well-sourced. The 3 to 5% direct card acceptance figure is consistent with industry estimates for Chinese B2B. The 180-day chargeback window is accurate for most major card schemes. 'Loan with a return policy' is metaphor, defensible. Main risk: the WF claim is broad ('CNY direct on the same rails') without naming the exact rail. A skeptic with card experience on Alibaba Trade Assurance will note that escrowed card payments DO work in some cases, so the 'you're going to get rejected' opener is slightly absolute.

skeptic_comments.text: I paid by card on Alibaba Trade Assurance lah. Worked fine. So 'going to get rejected' is wrong what.
3 to 5% where you get this number? Got source?
180 day chargeback for B2B? My card company never explain like that.
Who is WorldFirst? First time hearing. Got Malaysia license?
Same rails as Alipay? Means you also tier with Ant or what?

PH2.analysis: Four-point stack is structurally clean and each point maps to a real mechanism (acceptance rate, fee incidence, chargeback window, prevailing rails). The 2.95% fee figure matches Alibaba published rates. 'Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B' is accurate. Main risk: '50K order' as a hypothetical is fine but the supplier-bears-fee framing oversimplifies. On Trade Assurance, Alibaba absorbs some risk via escrow. Skeptic with Alipay experience may push back on 'rails they already use' as vague.

skeptic_comments.text: Supplier pays the 2.95% not me? Sure or not? On my Alibaba card transactions buyer side also charged.
Trade Assurance card payment got escrow lah. Not the same as direct card.
Where the 3 to 5% acceptance number come from? Show me the study.
Most factories never wired for Visa, true. But that's why I use Trade Assurance.
WorldFirst on Alipay rails? They got partnership or just messaging?

PH3.analysis: The flip (it's not the supplier, it's the category) is the strongest creative angle and defensible. Materials timeline (raw materials week one, dispute month five) is a real and underappreciated risk for suppliers. Main risk: '95% of Chinese factories don't even have card terminals' is a strong claim that overstates the source finding (the actual finding is bank card terminals are rare in B2B). Card acceptance via Alibaba escrow exists for many of those same factories indirectly. Skeptic may also push back on 'they run on Alipay and WeChat' for B2B factory wires, where T/T to corporate bank account is also very common.

skeptic_comments.text: My factory pays salary and ops via bank transfer lah, not WeChat. WeChat for retail mostly.
95% no terminal, where this come from? My supplier in Yiwu got QR code everywhere.
Material lose in month 5 chargeback ok this part interesting. Got real case?
Trade Assurance escrow handle the chargeback risk lah. Not all card payment same.
Who is WorldFirst? Why I trust you over Alibaba?

PH4.analysis: Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The retail-vs-wholesale category argument is genuinely good teaching and defensible. The sweater-dispute analogy reduces jargon. Timeline (week one materials, week eight ship, week twelve receive) is realistic for typical orders. The 2.95% supplier-side burden is consistent with Alibaba's published merchant fees. 'No supplier on earth' is the only overreach since some larger factories with international setup DO take cards. Main red flag remaining: 'CNY direct on the same rails' is still vague on the WF side.

skeptic_comments.text: Card built for retail, this part actually make sense. Good teaching.
No supplier on earth take card? My larger supplier in Shenzhen accept card no problem.
Week 12 still inside the 180 day chargeback, ok now I understand why supplier scared.
WorldFirst CNY direct on same rails, what rails exactly? Show me.
If supplier eat 2.95% how come some Alibaba sellers still happy with card?

PH5.analysis: The 3-thing stack is teachable and the math on a 12K order (360 fee) is internally consistent. 'Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B' is accurate. The 'protecting their business' framing is empathetic and lands well. Main red flag: 'supplier eats the 2.95% fee' is technically true for direct card acceptance but ignores Trade Assurance scenarios where Alibaba absorbs the dispute risk. A skeptic who's done Trade Assurance card payments will push back. 'They claw the money back' is dramatic but factually accurate for chargebacks.

skeptic_comments.text: 12K order 360 fee, math check out. But still on Alibaba I never pay 360 extra leh.
3 to 5% acceptance is direct card right? Trade Assurance got different model.
Supplier eat the 2.95%, where the source? My Alibaba transaction showed buyer fee.
Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst? Owned by Ant Group? Trust factor.
Protecting their business, this empathy line is good. First time I see content explain supplier side.

PG1.analysis: Same core mechanism as Hormozi set (3 to 5% acceptance, Alipay/WeChat dominance, 180-day chargeback). Softer 'worth knowing' close reduces the ad feel. 'Category never existed there' is a sharp insight but also a slight overstatement since cards do exist in Chinese consumer life, just not factory B2B. 'No 180-day risk for them' on WF side is a clean claim but a skeptic will want to understand the actual settlement mechanism.

skeptic_comments.text: Category never existed there? Cards exist in China lah, just not B2B factory.
3 to 5% where this come from? Got real data?
WorldFirst pay in CNY direct, no chargeback for them. Ok but how settlement work?
Who is WorldFirst leh, Ant Group? Trust depends on that.
Worth knowing, ok less ad feel than other versions. But still want proof.

PG2.analysis: 'Most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments' is a bold opinion framed as opinion, which is defensible Gary Vee voice. The supplier-side framing (less money + more risk for same order) is the sharpest insight in the set. Math holds: supplier eats 2.95%, takes 180-day chargeback risk. Main red flag: 'never been part of their world' is rhetorical and easy to challenge with one counter-example. Trade Assurance card workflow is a real exception.

skeptic_comments.text: Most overrated tool? Shipping cost overrate me lah, not card fee.
Trade Assurance card on Alibaba is fine for me. So 'not part of their world' is too strong.
Less money more risk same order, ok this framing actually clean.
How WorldFirst make money if no fee on supplier side? Got buyer side fee?
Show real comparison please. Card vs WorldFirst on same 12K transaction.

PG3.analysis: Hammer/wood metaphor is defensible reframing. The 10K PO math is internally consistent (2.95% = 300 bucks of supplier margin). 'Five months of their cash exposed' is a real and useful reframe of the chargeback window. Main red flags: 'of course they say no' is rhetorical and ignores the Trade Assurance exception. 'No 3% fee on their side' on WF is technically true but the skeptic will want to know what fee structure replaces it. 'Same rails they already use' remains vague.

skeptic_comments.text: 10K order 300 supplier margin, math check. But my supplier still accept on Trade Assurance.
Five months cash exposed, this is good reframe. Never thought from supplier POV.
WorldFirst no fee on supplier side, what about my side? Got top up min? Subscription?
Same rails they already use, which rails specifically? Alipay? Local bank? Show me.
Who is WorldFirst regulated by in Malaysia? BNM?

PG4.analysis: Strongest concept in the GV set. The B2C-vs-B2B category argument is genuinely good teaching. The timeline math (90-day production + 30-day shipping = 120 days, well inside the 180-day chargeback window) is accurate and defensible. Coffee shops/online stores/hotel examples make the retail framing concrete. Main risks: 'never meant for this' is rhetorical and a skeptic with Trade Assurance experience will note that Alibaba designed a card-with-escrow workflow that explicitly addresses this. 'Forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow' is sharp.

skeptic_comments.text: 90-day production 30-day shipping 180-day chargeback, math check. Good visual.
Card never meant for this, ok but Alibaba Trade Assurance designed card workflow for this exact case.
Coffee shops hotel rooms example, good. Made it click.
WorldFirst built differently, fine but show me how on a real transaction.
Who is WorldFirst? Need to know regulator before I trust.

PG5.analysis: Cleanest script in the set. The buyer-vs-supplier perspective contrast is empathetic and educational. 'Merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder' is technically accurate and a genuine teaching moment most buyers miss. 'A system 95% of their factory peers don't even use' is the only mild overreach. 'I see this every week' was softened to 'This happens every week' which removes the consultant-claim. Soft on specific numbers but rich on mechanism. Most defensible script in the GV set.

skeptic_comments.text: Merchant pays card fees not cardholder, ok this part I actually didn't know. Useful.
95% of factory peers don't use Visa, this is direct card right? Trade Assurance different.
Buyer vs supplier perspective, this is the best take in the set actually. Empathy wins.
This happens every week, bit much. Maybe every few months for me.
WorldFirst on Alipay WeChat local bank rails, which exactly? Partnership?
